TR_trigger+46+iScience__TR_trigger+47+Un.prediction:
Halogen additives: although flame retardancy improves from F < Cl < Br < I, iodine-based additives are rarely used because the same chemistry that makes them effective tends to introduce unacceptable side effects (e.g., poor electrochemical/chemical compatibility and instability), so the safety gain is traded off against reliability/performance risks.
Phosphate flame retardants: achieving strong flame suppression requires high loading; while large additions can push the self‑extinguishing time (SET) below the 6 s/g threshold, that high concentration degrades battery performance (reduced electrochemical performance due to dilution/changed electrolyte properties and associated failure modes such as increased impedance/poorer transport), so meeting the SET target trades off directly with capacity/power/cycle life.
TR_trigger+46+iScience__TR_trigger+47+Un.gold_answer: First, for halogen additives: although flame retardancy increases from F to I, iodine additives are impractical because the C–I bond is too weak to maintain chemical stability, eliminating the strongest option. Bromine or chlorine must be used instead, accepting lower flame retardancy. Second, for phosphate-based additives: achieving a nonflammable electrolyte (SET < 6 s/g) requires high additive concentrations, but redundant flame retardant amounts cause severe electrochemical performance degradation, including reduced chemical stability and inhibited ionic conductivity. Engineers must therefore balance flammability reduction against unacceptable capacity and conductivity losses, accepting either residual flammability or compromised electrochemical performance.
TR_trigger+46+iScience__TR_trigger+47+Un.reason: Captures both trade-offs: (1) halogen series trend and that iodine is impractical due to instability/compatibility issues (but misses the specific required mechanism: weak C–I bond), and (2) phosphate additives need high loading to reach SET < 6 s/g and that this degrades performance via worse transport/impedance (aligned with ionic conductivity degradation), though it omits the 6–20 s/g “less flammable” threshold detail.

TR_trigger+58+Journal_of_Power_Sources__.prediction: Not supported. Evidence indicates the overcharge mode changes anode-surface chemistry: high‑temperature CV overcharge drives strong fluorine enrichment (and oxygen increase) on the anode due to extensive electrolyte/LiPF6 decomposition and HF-driven SEI conversion to fluoride-rich products (e.g., LiF) under sustained high potential and heat. In CC overcharge, the response is different (less pronounced fluorine enrichment) because the cell is driven by constant current toward rapid failure/thermal runaway, changing the time–potential history and limiting the extent/type of SEI reconstruction and salt-decomposition products that accumulate on the anode.
TR_trigger+58+Journal_of_Power_Sources__.gold_answer: The generalization is not supported. Post-mortem evidence shows that CV and CC failure modes produce distinct anode surface chemistries. CV failure anodes exhibit higher relative fluorine percentages, while CC failure anodes show larger oxygen percentages. The difference is attributed to rate changes in reaction kinetics: high voltage (CV mode) drives reactions that preferentially increase fluorine-containing decomposition products, whereas high current (CC mode) drives kinetics that increase oxygen-containing products. Therefore, the ~4x fluorine enrichment observed after high-temperature CV overcharge is specific to that mode and cannot be generalized to CC overcharge failure. Attributing identical fluorine enrichment to CC failure would constitute a regime transfer error.
TR_trigger+58+Journal_of_Power_Sources__.reason: Correctly rejects the generalization and explains CV-specific fluorine enrichment via high-potential electrolyte/LiPF6 decomposition and differing current/voltage time histories. However, it misses the key required contrast that CC failure anodes show higher oxygen percentages (oxygen-rich products) and does not clearly frame the mechanism as voltage-vs-current driven kinetic rate differences leading to oxygen enrichment in CC.
